name: Justice Defenders
mission: Defending justice with defenceless communities in Africa through legal education, training, and practice.
theoryOfChange: Justice Defenders has a published 'Our Theory of Change' document available on their official website under 'The Justice Defenders Way'. This framework outlines their 2025-2027 strategy, detailing how they aim to grow their movement by shifting power within prisons and reshaping justice systems. Their approach involves expanding legal education and practice, creating pathways from incarceration to advocacy, and advancing relational systems change. Specifically, they focus on three key areas: Education (training paralegals and delivering law degrees), Practice (advising clients, preparing cases, and pursuing public interest litigation), and Convening (bringing together allies for shared reflection and service). The document states their goal is to establish the world's first law firm of lived experience, transforming understanding of the law and who practices it.

The overall score rationale mentions high financial transparency but doesn't explicitly acknowledge the high CEO compensation and unsustainable spending, which are key factors contributing to the moderate score.

dimension_explanations.alignment: Justice Defenders addresses the urgent cause of legal advocacy in underserved regions [3]. While it offers opportunities for general charitable giving or sadaqah [8], it has a low donor fit for those seeking specific religious alignment, resulting in a medium alignment score of 27/50.
dimension_explanations.impact: The organization focuses on capacity building by training paralegals and facilitating law degrees for prisoners and staff, resulting in numerous releases through legal support and alternative dispute resolution [3][5]. While it maintains a high Charity Navigator score of 91.0/100 [1], its program expense ratio is 58.5%, and the cost per beneficiary is calculated at approximately $27.22 [2].

strengths.detail: The organization holds a <cite id="4">91/100 overall score from Charity Navigator</cite> and has earned the <cite id="9">Candid Platinum Seal of Transparency</cite>.
In 2024 alone, the organization reported that <cite id="3">15,338 prisoners were released</cite> and <cite id="10">2,032 individuals were released through ADR</cite>.
Justice Defenders demonstrates high fundraising efficiency, spending only <cite id="11">$0.12 to raise every $1</cite> in contributions.

strengths_deep_dive: Justice Defenders distinguishes itself through a highly sustainable capacity-building model. By training <cite id="12">370 paralegals</cite> within the prison system, they create a self-sustaining cycle of legal aid. Their academic rigor is evidenced by <cite id="13">47 law degree graduates</cite> who have completed prestigious programs while incarcerated. This model not only provides immediate legal relief but also addresses long-term systemic injustice by professionalizing legal services for the most marginalized populations in Africa.
rich_narrative.summary: Justice Defenders is a specialized non-profit organization that empowers incarcerated individuals and prison staff in Africa through legal education and training to ensure fair access to justice. Founded in <cite id="1">2007</cite>, the organization addresses systemic legal gaps by equipping those within the prison system to serve as paralegals and legal advocates. In 2023, the organization reported <cite id="2">total revenue of $1,868,837</cite>, supporting a unique model that has facilitated the <cite id="3">release of over 15,000 prisoners</cite> through legal support and alternative dispute resolution. The charity maintains a <cite id="4">91/100 overall score from Charity Navigator</cite>, reflecting high standards of accountability. Their work is particularly impactful in <cite id="5">Kenya and Uganda</cite>, where they provide <cite id="6">University of London law degree programs</cite> to inmates and staff, transforming prisons into hubs of legal practice. Despite its specialized focus, the organization operates with a <cite id="7">program expense ratio of 58.5%</cite> according to recent filings, while maintaining <cite id="8">9.2 months of working capital</cite> to ensure operational continuity in challenging environments.
